coredata.dc:title: Hollow carbon spheres anchored with nitrogen-doped carbon dots for high-performance supercapacitors
coredata.dc:creator: Dai, Jiaxu
Li, Guochang
Hu, Yaoping
Han, Lei
coredata.dc:description: As an emerging class of electrode materials, carbon dots are able to provide large specific surface area, abundant active sites and plentiful pseudocapacitive groups, which can effectively improve the electrochemical performance of supercapacitors. Herein, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) are anchored on hollow carbon spheres (HCS) to construct novel carbon-based composites (NCDs@HCS) with hierarchical porous structures. Compared to the original HCS, the optimal NCDs@HCS sample has an ultrahigh specific surface area of 2163 m2 g−1 and possesses superior capacitance characteristics, including a significantly increased specific capacitance of 314 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and an excellent cycling stability with 99.44 % capacitance retention after 10,000 cycles in a three-electrode system. When applied as a symmetric supercapacitor, the NCDs@HCS composite exhibits an enhanced energy density of 37.9 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 900 W kg−1. Such a performance surpasses that of most other carbon-based materials. The outstanding electrochemical behaviors of NCDs@HCS benefit from the unique morphology of HCS and the rich electron-donating regions provided by NCDs, which facilitate the mass transport and charge transfer. This work offers a new strategy to design advanced porous carbons for efficient energy storage.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Hollow carbon spheres
Porous carbon-based composite
Supercapacitor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province ( LQ20E020005 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo City ( 2021J075 ), and the K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of NCDs; (b, c) SEM images of RF@SiO2 and NCDs@HCS-3; (d, e) TEM and (f) HRTEM images of NCDs@HCS-3; (g) HAADF image of a single NCDs@HCS-3 and corresponding EDS elemental mapping of C, N and O.
(a) XRD patterns, (b) Raman spectra, (c) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms, and (d) pore size distributions of HCS and NCDs@HCS-3 measured by Density Functional Theory (DFT) method.
XPS spectra of NCDs@HCS-3: (a) survey; (b) C 1s; (c) N 1s and (d) O 1s. Inset in (a): atomic concentrations of different elements in NCDs@HCS-3.

(a) CV curves at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1, (c) GCD curves under a current density of 1 A g−1, (e) specific capacitances at various current densities, and (f) Nyquist plots of HCS and different NCDs@HCS composites; (b) CV curves at various scan rates, (d) GCD curves at various current densities, and (g) cycling stability of NCDs@HCS-3.

(a) CV curves, (b) GCD curves, (c) specific capacitances calculated by GCD, (d) Nyquist plots, and (e) cycling stability of NCDs@HCS-3 in a two-electrode system; (f) Ragone plots of NCDs@HCS-3 in a two-electrode system and other symmetric devices in the previous works.
A schematic illustration for the preparation of NCDs@HCS.
A comparison of electrochemical performances of various carbon materials used in supercapacitors.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The value in the brackets refers to the current density (A g−1).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: DHCM: double-layered mesopore-rich hollow carbon microspheres.

head.ce:title: Hollow carbon spheres anchored with nitrogen-doped carbon dots for high-performance supercapacitors

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering, Ningbo University, Ningbo, Zhejiang, 315211, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Advanced Mass Spectrometry and Molecular Analysis of Zhejiang Province, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Y. Hu, Key Laboratory of Advanced Mass Spectrometry and Molecular Analysis of Zhejiang Province, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Advanced Mass Spectrometry and Molecular Analysis of Zhejiang Province

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As an emerging class of electrode materials, carbon dots are able to provide large specific surface area, abundant active sites and plentiful pseudocapacitive groups, which can effectively improve the electrochemical performance of supercapacitors. Herein, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) are anchored on hollow carbon spheres (HCS) to construct novel carbon-based composites (NCDs@HCS) with hierarchical porous structures. Compared to the original HCS, the optimal NCDs@HCS sample has an ultrahigh specific surface area of 2163 m2 g−1 and possesses superior capacitance characteristics, including a significantly increased specific capacitance of 314 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and an excellent cycling stability with 99.44 % capacitance retention after 10,000 cycles in a three-electrode system. When applied as a symmetric supercapacitor, the NCDs@HCS composite exhibits an enhanced energy density of 37.9 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 900 W kg−1. Such a performance surpasses that of most other carbon-based materials. The outstanding electrochemical behaviors of NCDs@HCS benefit from the unique morphology of HCS and the rich electron-donating regions provided by NCDs, which facilitate the mass transport and charge transfer. This work offers a new strategy to design advanced porous carbons for efficient energy storage.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitors, a kind of efficient and sustainable energy storage devices, have garnered considerable interest owing to their high power density, fast charge-discharge capabilities, and excellent cycling stability [1–5]. Supercapacitors can be categorized into two types: pseudocapacitors (PsCs) and electric double layer capacitors (EDLCs) [6,7]. PsCs, relying on fast Faraday redox reactions within various materials such as transition metal hydroxides, transition metal oxides and transition metal dichalcogenides, can offer relatively high energy densities [8–12]. However, the poor cycling stability and low conductivity hinder their practical applications [13]. On the other hand, EDLCs store and transfer energy through the formation of a double electric layer between the electrodes and electrolyte, which exhibit long cycle lifetime and rapid charge transfer with good retention rate [14,15]. Based on energy storage mechanism, ideal electrode materials for EDLCs require suitable channels for diffusion of electrolyte, rich active sites for high charge storage, and good conductivity for fast electron transport [16]. Carbon materials meet the requirements and are usually the preferred choices in EDLCs due to their excellent conductivity, high specific surface area, favorable chemical stability, low cost and easy availability [17–19].

Among various carbon materials, hollow carbon spheres (HCS) composed of carbon shells and internal cavities exhibit unique features [20]. The hollow interior of HCS can serve as a reservoir or microreactor, while the abundant porous channels in the carbon shell are able to facilitate the diffusion of ions [21]. Nevertheless, the utilization of HCS in supercapacitors is still hampered by insufficient energy storage sites, sluggish ion diffusion kinetics, and slow electron transport [22].

Carbon dots (CDs) possessing ultrasmall sizes (diameters <10 nm), multiple defects, accessible edges and good water dispersibility, have emerged as rising stars in EDLC electrode materials [23–27]. Additionally, doping heteroatoms like N, P, and S into CDs can induce pseudocapacitive activity and enhance surface wettability, leading to the increase of capacitance and acceleration of charge-discharge kinetics [28]. However, CDs themselves tend to agglomerate because of strong π–π interaction, which results in the decrease of surface area and loss of active sites, and thus brings about the limited capacitance [29]. To solve the problem, combining CDs with other carbon materials to construct composites may not only inherit the advantages of each component, but also acquire the encouraging synergistic effect, thereby achieving the superior electrochemical properties [30]. For example, Xiong group synthesized hierarchical porous carbons by calcinating CDs-polyacrylamide hydrogel composites, which showed improved performances in energy density, power density, and cycling life [31]. Wang et al. modified proanthocyanidins with CDs to prepare mesoporous carbons that could be applied as supercapacitors with enhanced light-assisted capacitance [32]. Despite some impressive cases, research on CDs-decorated carbon composites for excellent supercapacitor electrodes is still in an early stage and need further exploration.

In this work, we design a novel carbon hybrid (NCDs@HCS) by anchoring nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) on HCS as illustrated in Scheme 1 . The HCS materials were derived from resorcinol-formaldehyde@silica spheres (RF@SiO2). The incorporation of NCDs in HCS provides the electron-rich regions, increases the active sites, enlarges the specific surface area, enhances the wettability and affords additional pseudocapacitance, thus achieving an improved specific capacitance of 314 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 in a three-electrode system. Such a value is 82.8 % higher than that of the original HCS without NCDs modification. Also importantly, NCDs@HCS keeps an extremely robust cycling life with capacitance retention of nearly 100 % after 10,000 cycles. Furthermore, the assembled symmetric capacitor using NCDs@HCS shows greater electrochemical performances than those of other carbon-based materials. These results demonstrate that the newly developed NCDs@HCS composite is a better option for application in high-performance supercapacitors.

Ethylenediamine (EDA), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 wt%), ammonia solution (NH3·H2O, 25–28 wt%), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), resorcinol, formaldehyde solution, sulfuric acid (H2SO4), hydrofluoric acid (HF), hydrochloric acid (HCl), potassium hydroxide (KOH), potassium chloride (KCl), and ethanol were obtained from Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd. All other chemicals were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Co., Ltd.

NCDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal strategy. Briefly, 3 mL of ethylenediamine and 6 mL of hydrogen peroxide aqueous solution (30 wt%) were added to a beaker containing 21 mL of deionized water. After 2-min stirring, the mixture was transferred into a 50-mL Teflon-sealed autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. The resulting NCD dispersion was passed through a 0.22-μm membrane filter and dialyzed (molecular weight cut off 1000 Da) with deionized water for purification.

In a typical run, 2.5 mL of ammonia solution (25–28 wt%) as a base catalyst was added to a round-bottom flask containing 64 mL of ethanol/water mixture (volume ratio of ethanol to water = 2:1) and then stirred at 30 °C for 30 min. Subsequently, the following reagents were successively added to the above flask at different times: 2.5 mL of tetraethyl orthosilicate was added immediately, 0.4 g of resorcinol was added after 1 h, and 0.56 mL of formaldehyde solution was added after another 10 min. The mixture was stirred at 30 °C for 18 h and then transferred into a 100-mL Teflon-sealed autoclave. Heating the reactants at 100 °C for 24 h resulted in the production of resorcinol formaldehyde polymer coated SiO2 spheres (RF@SiO2). The RF@SiO2 powder was then annealed at 800 °C for 1 h under Ar atmosphere in a tubular furnace with a heating rate of 5 °C min−1. Afterward, the carbonized products were acidified with H2SO4 for 4 h, etched with HF, washed several times with water and ethanol until pH was returned to neutral, and dried under vacuum to obtain a composite called pre-HCS. In the next step, pre-HCS was mixed with KOH (1:2 by mass) and KCl (KCl was added as 20 % molar of KOH) for activation at 850 °C for 2 h in Ar atmosphere. Finally, HCS with a porous shell was obtained by washing the activated sample with dilute HCl, water and ethanol.

0.05 g of RF@SiO2 and a certain amount of NCDs (0.05, 0.10, 0.15 and 0.20 g) were introduced into deionized water. The total volume of the solution was maintained at 30 mL. After sonication for 30 min, the mixture was transferred into a Teflon-sealed autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 12 h. The resulting suspension was centrifuged to collect the precipitate. It should be mentioned that due to the poor dispersibility of RF@SiO2 and high dispersibility of NCDs in water, most of NCDs remained in the aqueous phase, and only a small part of the added NCDs were attached to RF@SiO2 (0.0106, 0.0172, 0.0227, and 0.0267 g of NCDs were finally attached to RF@SiO2). Using the same ways as described above, carbonization of the precipitate followed by removal of SiO2 led to the production of pre-NCDs@HCS-X, where X represented the mass ratio of the initially added NCDs to RF@SiO2, i.e., 1, 2, 3, and 4. NCDs@HCS-X was obtained by further activating pre-NCDs@HCS-X.

Morphology and microstructures of the materials were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S-4800) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai F20). Crystal phases of the samples were identified by an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance) with Cu Ka radiation. Raman spectra were measured using a 532 nm laser on LabRam HR Evolution. Elemental compositions and valence states of the products were analyzed by an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha) with Al Ka radiation. N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of the samples were obtained using a Micromeritics ASAP 2460 instrument at 77 K. Specific surface areas were determined by the Brunauer-Emmett-teller (BET) method and pore size distributions were measured by the Density Functional Theory (DFT) model. Photoluminescence spectra of the NCD dispersion were monitored by a Hitachi F-4600 spectrophotometer.

Both the three-electrode and two-electrode configurations were applied to investigate the electrochemical properties of the samples on a CHI660C electrochemical workstation (Shanghai ChenHua Instruments Co., Ltd). The tests were carried out in 6 M KOH solution at ambient temperature. The sample, acetylene black and polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) were mixed in a weight ratio of 8:1:1 using N-methyl pyrrolidone (NMP) as the solvent to form a slurry. Subsequently, the slurry was uniformly coated on a 1 cm × 1 cm nickel foam substrate, dried and subjected to electrochemical testing.

In the three-electrode system, the specific capacitance of the electrodes was determined by analyzing the charge/discharge curves with the following equation:
C m
=
I d
× ∆ t
∆ V × m
where C m (F g−1) is the specific capacitance, I d (mA) is the constant current, Δt (s) is the discharge time, ΔV (V) is the discharge voltage range and m (mg) is the mass loading of the active material on a single electrode.

In the two-electrode system, the specific capacitance was calculated by analyzing the charge/discharge curves with the following equation:
C m
=
2 ×
I d
× ∆ t
∆ V × m

The energy density (E, Wh kg−1) and power density (P, W kg−1) of the symmetric supercapacitor were calculated using the following equation:
E =
C m
× ∆
V 2
2 × 4 × 3.6
P =
3600 × E
∆ t
where C m (F g−1) is the specific capacitance based on the two electrodes.

Hydrothermal treatment of ethylenediamine with H2O2 led to the production of NCDs. As shown in Fig. S1, the resulting solution emits bright blue fluorescence under a UV lamp and exhibits an excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior, which indicate the formation of NCDs [33]. TEM technique was employed to observe the morphology of the product. It can be seen in Fig. 1a that NCDs are nearly spherical particles with diameters of 1–3 nm; they are randomly dispersed without apparent aggregation. Lattice fringe is not distinguishable in NCDs (inset in Fig. 1a), revealing their amorphous nature. The XRD and XPS results (Fig. S2 & Fig. S3) demonstrate that NCDs contain aromatic carbon and CN clusters in the core and are functionalized with plentiful groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino on the surface. We used SiO2 as a template to prepare HCS. Fig. 1b shows an SEM image of SiO2 nanospheres coated with resorcinol-formaldehyde polymers (denoted as RF@SiO2). RF@SiO2 spheres have uniform sizes with an average diameter of ≈100 nm, and their surfaces are quite smooth (inset in Fig. 1b). RF@SiO2 was then anchored with different amount of NCDs and turned to be NCDs@HCS-X (where X stands for the mass ratio of the initially added NCDs to RF@SiO2) using the methods described in the experimental section. In the SEM image of NCDs@HCS-3 (Fig. 1c), it is found that the composite retains the original morphology and size of RF@SiO2, but the surface becomes much coarser and contains some small pores (inset in Fig. 1c). These changes are caused by surface decoration of RF@SiO2 with NCDs, acid corrosion of SiO2 core, and KOH activation for pore creation during the synthetic processes. The TEM images of NCDs@HCS-3 (Fig. 1d & e) display that the composite contains a large cavity with diameter of ≈80 nm and a thin shell with thickness of ≈10 nm. In the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 1f), a number of NCDs (marked with white circles) anchored on the HCS shell can be distinguished. The high-angle annular dark field (HAADF) image of a single NCDs@HCS-3 and the corresponding EDS elemental mapping images (Fig. 1g) demonstrate that besides C and O, element of N is uniformly distributed on the shell of the composite, which further confirms the successful anchoring of NCDs on HCS (the original HCS does not contain the element of N; so, N in the carbon composite must come from NCDs).

XRD and Raman spectroscopy were carried out to investigate the crystal structure and graphitization degree of the sample. Fig. 2a shows that NCDs@HCS-3 exhibits two broad peaks at 23° and 43.5°, corresponding to the (002) and (101) planes of amorphous carbon, respectively [34]. Compared with HCS, the (002) plane of NCDs@HCS-3 shifts to a lower value (from 23.4° to 22.7°), indicating an enlarged interlayer distance in the composite due to the incorporation of numerous N atoms [35]. In the Raman spectrum of NCDs@HCS-3 (Fig. 2b), a disordered D band at 1342 cm−1 and a crystalline G band at 1580 cm−1 are presented. The intensity ratio of D to G band (ID/IG) is 1.11, higher than that of HCS (1.03), suggesting that the combination of NCDs introduces an increased number of structural defects [36,37], which provides more active sites for enhancing the supercapacitor capacitance. Contact angle measurements were performed on HCS and NCDs@HCS-3 utilizing a 6 mol L−1 KOH electrolyte (Fig. S4). The anchoring of NCDs on HCS results in a marked decrease in contact angle, indicating the improved wettability, which is helpful to achieve a higher ion-accessible surface area [38]. N2 adsorption/desorption test was conducted to evaluate the pore features of the material. As depicted in Fig. 2c, NCDs@HCS-3 has a type-IV characteristic with a type-H4 hysteresis loop. The sharp rise at low relative pressure (P/P0 < 0.02) denotes the presence of abundant micropores [39]. The noticeable hysteresis loop in the intermediate relative pressure range (P/P0 = 0.4–0.9) suggests that there are lots of mesopores inside NCDs@HCS-3 [35]. The extended tails at high relative pressure (P/P0 > 0.9) signifies the existence of large pores associated with cavity structure [13]. In comparison, the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherm of HCS does not exhibit an apparent hysteresis loop, which reveals that after anchoring NCDs on HCS, the composite contains much more mesopores. This analysis is further supported by the curves of pore size distribution (Fig. 2d), in which we can see that compared with HCS, NCDs@HCS-3 has a greater number of micropores and mesopores. It is deduced that the partial thermal decomposition of NCDs in the composite creates a plenty of small pores [32]. The specific surface area of NCDs@HCS-3 reaches up to 2163 m2 g−1, 52 % higher than that of HCS (1423 m2 g−1) and surpassing the values of most reported porous carbons. The hierarchical porous structure and remarkably improved specific surface area of NCDs@HCS-3 provide more ion storage sites and facilitate ion transport and diffusion, which is propitious to enhance the electrochemical performance [40].

The elemental compositions and chemical states of NCDs@HCS-3 were analyzed by XPS. The full-scan spectrum (Fig. 3a) demonstrates that NCDs@HCS-3 predominantly consists of carbon (93.2 %), nitrogen (2.4 %) and oxygen (4.4 %). The high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 3b) can be deconvoluted into four peaks at 284.7, 285.9, 286.8, and 288.8 eV, corresponding to CC/CC, CN, CO, and CO bonds, respectively [41]. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 3c) displays pyridinic-N at 398.2 eV, pyrrolic-N at 399.4 eV, graphitic-N at 401.1 eV, and oxide-N at 402.3 eV, proving that the N atoms from NCDs have been doped into the carbon network of the composite [42]. Pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N can increase the specific capacitance, while graphitic-N is useful to enhance the electrical conductivity [30]. In the O 1s spectrum (Fig. 3d), three oxygen signals are presented, including OCO at 531.5 eV, CO at 532.8 eV, and COH/COC at 535.5 eV [43]. The rich N atoms, hydrophilic functional groups, as well as hierarchical porous structure and high specific surface area, may endow NCDs@HCS-3 excellent performances in supercapacitors.

The electrochemical performances of HCS and the NCDs@HCS composites containing different amount of NCDs were evaluated in a three-electrode system using a 6 M KOH aqueous electrolyte. As shown in Fig. 4a, the cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of all the samples measured at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1 exhibit a similar slightly skewed rectangular shape, and a redox peak at approximately −0.67 V becomes more obvious with the increased amount of NCDs, demonstrating that the dominating feature of all the materials is EDLC, and the introduction of NCDs bring about the additional PsC characteristic [44]. Among these materials, NCDs@HCS-3 has the largest CV area, so it is used for a detailed study. Fig. 4b records the CV curves of NCDs@HCS-3 at various scan rates of 10–200 mV s−1, in which we can see that as the scan rate rises, the CV shape of NCDs@HCS-3 remains unchanged, indicating the stable capacitance behavior. All the galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) profiles of the samples under a current density of 1 A g−1 are slightly distorted from isosceles triangle (Fig. 4c), which further confirms the combination of EDLC and PsC contributions [44]. According to the discharge curves, NCDs@HCS-3 has the highest specific capacitance of 314 F g−1 at 1 A g−1, far superior to that of the original HCS (171.8 F g−1 at 1 A g−1), suggesting that the anchored NCDs notably improves ion transportability and accessibility. Fig. 4d displays the GCD profiles of NCDs@HCS-3 at different current densities. The specific capacitance of NCDs@HCS-3 decreases from 327.5 to 272 F g−1 when the current density increases from 0.5 to 10 A g−1, meaning that the diffusion rate of the electrolyte ions drops during the accelerated charge-discharge process. For HCS and other NCDs@HCS composites, their CV and GCD curves at different conditions are depicted in Figs. S5–S8. Fig. 4e shows that all the samples have good capacitance retentions in the range of 68 %–83 % while the current density increases from 0.5 to 10 A g−1, among which NCDs@HCS-3 possesses the largest value, demonstrating the best rate capability [45]. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was measured to test the ionic kinetics of the electrode materials. Fig. 4f shows the Nyquist plots of different samples, among which NCDs@HCS-3 presents the lowest solution resistance (Rs) of 0.32 Ω and the minimum charge transfer resistance (Rct) of 0.11 Ω, indicating the best conductivity. The nearly vertical line in the low-frequency region reveals that NCDs@HCS-3 has a small ion diffusion resistance, which is able to facilitate ion transport. The NCDs@HCS-3 electrode material exhibits an outstanding cycling stability, which is reflected by the evidences that the capacitance retention rate keeps 99.44 % after 10,000 cycles at a high current density of 10 A g−1, and the Coulombic efficiency remains at approximately 100 % (Fig. 4g). The insets in Fig. 4g show that the charge-discharge curves in the initial and last 10 cycles are almost identical to each other, implying the high reversibility. The morphology of NCDs@HCS-3 after 10,000 cycling is observed by SEM and compared with that of the material before use. As shown in Fig. S9, nearly no morphology change occurs after continuous use of NCDs@HCS-3, further indicating its good stability. The above tests collectively demonstrate a significant improvement in the electrochemical performance of the NCDs@HCS-3 sample, which is attributed to the positive synergistic effect between NCDs and HCS. This synergistic effect probably comes from the facts that (1) NCDs, uniformly anchored on HCS, can act as “electron transfer highways”, and the doped N atoms can bring more defects and better wettability, which help to improve the specific capacitance; (2) The hollow structure of carbon spheres can serve as a storage tank for electrolytes, shortening the ion transport distance; (3) The hierarchical porous structure and large specific surface area can increase storage sites, effectively promoting ion diffusion and electron transfer. Fig. S10 illustrates the principle of improving the electrochemical properties for NCDs@HCS. It should be mentioned that incorporation of appropriate amount of NCDs in HCS is beneficial to enhance the electrochemical performances of the material; however, addition of too many NCDs may result in their self-agglomeration, which decreases the specific surface area of the final NCDs@HCS composite, leading to the poorer electrochemical performances (for NCDs@HCS-4). Thus, the quantity of added NCDs should be carefully controlled. NCDs have also been annealed and activated alone as NCDs@HCS-3. In the SEM image (Fig. S11a), it is found that the material directly derived from NCDs (denoted as MNCDs) possesses an irregular block morphology, which is rather different from the nanostructured NCDs@HCS-3. With much lower specific surface area, the electrochemical performances of MNCDs are significantly worse than NCDs@HCS-3 (Fig. S11b & c). Table 1 summarizes the electrochemical performances of various porous carbon materials in three-electrode supercapacitors reported in recent years. Obviously, the NCDs@HCS-3 electrode material shows a superior performance, surpassing that of most other carbon materials with different morphologies [19,36,46–54], such as carbon spheres [19], carbon fibers [46], carbon nanosheets [47], and carbon nanoblocks [48].

To evaluate the potential application of the as-synthesized composite, a symmetric supercapacitor was fabricated using two identical NCDs@HCS-3 electrodes in 6 M KOH electrolyte. All the CV curves of NCDs@HCS-3 at different scan rates of 10–500 mV s−1 show a quasi-rectangular shape, and negligible distortion is observed when the scan rate increases (Fig. 5a & Fig. S12a), demonstrating the high reversibility and rate capability. All the GCD profiles at various current densities of 0.5–100 A g−1 exhibit a quasi-isosceles triangle shape (Fig. 5b & Fig. S12b), which reveals that NCDs@HCS-3 mainly has the EDLC characteristic. The specific capacitance of the symmetric supercapacitor is calculated to be 273.2 F g−1 at 1 A g A g−1, and still maintains a high value of 204 F g−1 at 60 A g−1 (Fig. 5c). The Nyquist plot in Fig. 5d reveals that the Rs and Rct of NCDs@HCS-3 are 0.65 Ω and 0.38 Ω, respectively, and the slope of the line in the low-frequency region is steep, indicating efficient and rapid ion migration [42]. After 10,000 continuous charge-discharge cycles, NCDs@HCS-3 keeps a capacity retention of 90.83 %, and the coulombic efficiency retains nearly 100 % (Fig. 5e), indicating the outstanding cycling stability. In the Ragone plots (Fig. 5f), it can be seen that the NCDs@HCS-3 based symmetric capacitor delivers an energy density of 37.9 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 900 W kg−1, and even at the much higher power density of 36 kW kg−1, it still has an energy density of 30 Wh kg−1. These values are significantly larger than those reported in the previous works [13,19,37,45,46,48,52,55–58], such as N-doped hollow mesoporous carbon spheres symmetric supercapacitor (energy density of 17.1 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 298.9 W kg−1) [45], N-doped porous carbon microcapsule symmetric supercapacitor (energy density of 4.7 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 320 W kg−1) [55], and N-rich porous carbons symmetric supercapacitor (energy density of 33.9 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 809.9 W kg−1) [56]. Therefore, NCDs@HCS-3 is a promising competitor to classical carbon-based materials for practical use in powerful supercapacitors.

In summary, we have prepared a novel carbon-based electrode material by anchoring NCDs onto RF-derived HCS. The ultrasmall NCDs with abundant structural defects and functional groups not only greatly increase the number of active sites in the NCDs@HCS composite, but also provide favorable interfaces for interactions between electrolyte and electrodes, thus enhancing ion adsorption and facilitating electron transport. The optimal NCDs@HCS sample exhibits a high specific capacitance of 314 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and a stable cycling life with 99.44 % retention after 10,000 cycles in a three-electrode system. Moreover, the symmetric two-electrode capacitor based on NCDs@HCS shows a large energy density of 37.9 Wh kg−1 at a high power density of 900 W kg−1 in alkaline electrolyte. This work provides an effective method to improve the electrochemical performance of carbon materials for potential applications in energy storage and beyond.

Jiaxu Dai: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Validation, Writing – original draft. Guochang Li: Investigation, Methodology. Yaoping Hu: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Lei Han: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (LQ20E020005), the Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo City (2021J075), and the K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.est.2024.110640.
